coredata.dc:title: Optical and surface plasmon resonance sensing properties for chitosan/carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots thin film
coredata.dc:creator: Ramdzan, Nur Syahira Md
Fen, Yap Wing
Omar, Nur Alia Sheh
Anas, Nur Ain Asyiqin
Daniyal, Wan Mohd Ebtisyam Mustaqim Mohd
Saleviter, Silvan
Zainudin, Afiq Azri
coredata.dc:description: In this study, chitosan/carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots thin film was developed. Its optical and surface plasmon resonance sensing properties were explored. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy proved the presence of OH bond, CH bond, CO bond, and CO bond in chitosan/carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots thin film. The optical properties of the composite thin film were studied using UV–vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy. The chitosan/carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots thin film showed the high absorbance value with an optical band gap of 4.012 eV. From photoluminescence spectroscopy, red light emission spectra are emitted due to the absorption peak of about 725 nm to 775 nm that corresponds to π→π* transitions of CO bond. The surface morphology of thin film was observed using atomic force microscopy indicates a granular structure with an average height of 18.4 nm of the composite thin film. The experimental surface plasmon resonance curves were obtained for different concentration in a range from 0 to 100 ppm of Hg2+ solution. A linear relationship between the shifts of resonance angle for chitosan/carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots thin film in contact with Hg2+ solution with a sensitivity of 0.00062° ppm−1 has been observed. These results confirmed that the chitosan/carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots thin film shows potential for the detection of Hg2+ using surface plasmon resonance technique.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical properties
Graphene
Chitosan
Quantum dots
Surface plasmon resonance
Sensing
Mercury ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SPR signal for Hg2+ on gold single layer
SPR signal for Hg2+ using Cs/CGQDs on gold surface

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graph of the combined infrared radiation spectrum for different composite thin films.
Absorbance spectrum of chitosan, CGQDs, and Cs/CGGDs thin film.
Optical band gap for chitosan thin film.
Optical band gap for CGQDs thin film.
Optical band gap for Cs/CGQDs thin film.
Photoluminescence spectra of chitosan, CGQDs, and Cs/CGQDs thin film.
The SPR curve for gold layer in contact with deionized water.
The SPR curve for gold/Cs/CGQDs thin film in contact with deionized water.
(a) and (b): The SPR curves for gold/Cs/CGQDs thin film in contact with different concentration of Hg2+ solution (0.5–100 ppm).
The resonance angle shift of gold thin film in contact with different Hg2+ concentration.
The resonance angle shift of gold/Cs-CGQDs thin film in contact with different Hg2+ concentration.
AFM images of Cs/CGQDs thin film (a) before and (b) after in contact with Hg2+ solution.
Characteristics frequencies of Cs/CGQDs thin film.
Energy band gap of chitosan, CGQDs, and Cs/CGQDs thin film.
The resonance angle ( θ ) and shift of resonance angle ( Δ θ ) for different concentration of Hg2+ solution in contact of gold/Cs/GQDs thin film.

row.entry: Shift of Resonance Angle, Δ θ (degree)

head.ce:title: Optical and surface plasmon resonance sensing properties for chitosan/carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots thin film

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400, UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Devices Laboratory, Institute of Advanced Technology, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400, UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400, UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, chitosan/carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots thin film was developed. Its optical and surface plasmon resonance sensing properties were explored. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy proved the presence of OH bond, CH bond, CO bond, and CO bond in chitosan/carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots thin film. The optical properties of the composite thin film were studied using UV–vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy. The chitosan/carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots thin film showed the high absorbance value with an optical band gap of 4.012 eV. From photoluminescence spectroscopy, red light emission spectra are emitted due to the absorption peak of about 725 nm to 775 nm that corresponds to π→π* transitions of CO bond. The surface morphology of thin film was observed using atomic force microscopy indicates a granular structure with an average height of 18.4 nm of the composite thin film. The experimental surface plasmon resonance curves were obtained for different concentration in a range from 0 to 100 ppm of Hg2+ solution. A linear relationship between the shifts of resonance angle for chitosan/carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots thin film in contact with Hg2+ solution with a sensitivity of 0.00062° ppm−1 has been observed. These results confirmed that the chitosan/carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots thin film shows potential for the detection of Hg2+ using surface plasmon resonance technique.

ce:section.ce:para: With continuing advances in science and technology, semiconductor-based thin film has become a focus of attraction of researchers. The thin film is a layer of material deposited on glass slip with a thickness ranging from 10−6 m and above. Thin film has distinct advantages over thick film due to the properties of the thin film that tightly bound to particle size, shape and surface properties [1]. Other than that, the structural and optical properties depend and can be modified by the deposition thin film process [2]. As deposition conditions apply to thin film and plasma enhancement, crystalline phase composition can be varied to a certain extent.

There are various methods being developed for depositing thin film including evaporative methods, glow-discharge process, gas-phase chemical process, liquid-phase chemical techniques and dip coating [3,4]. However, these methods suffer from a severe drawback such as complex operation, expensive equipment, and expert operators is needed [4]. To overcome the aforementioned drawback, spin coating technique has been used to deposit a layer of material on glass film. Spin coater or simply spinner is an instrument that used in process of spin coating. This method produces uniform of deposition on thin film where a small amount of coating material is applied on the center of the substrate and being rotated at high speed in order to spread the coating material by centrifugal force. The rotation has proceeded until the point when the thickness of a thin film is achieved as desired. The applied solvent is usually volatile and simultaneously evaporates. Thus, the thickness of the thin film depends on the angular speed of spinning [3].

In the recent study, graphene-based materials thin film have been arisen and continuously attracted attention from the scientist. Graphene is a single layer of graphite or in other words, have one layer of carbon that bonded by sp2 bond in a honeycomb lattice that can be found in metallic substrate [5]. This unique material has unique physical properties such as high surface area, electrically high conductivity, high transparency, and high thermal stability [6]. In addition, linear energy-momentum relationship and zero bandgaps are the most unique properties of graphene [5]. However, zero bandgaps will give impacts on zero results of optical luminescence that been observed in pristine graphene. Scientists have engineered bandgap in graphene by form graphene quantum dots (GQDs). GQDs is nanometer-scale semiconductor crystal that consists of sp2–sp2 carbon bond. GQDs based sensor will achieve high performance due to its novel properties. The modification of electron behaviour due to quantum confinement, resulted in the reduction of GQDs size that comparable to exciton Bohr radius and it is the difference between GQDs and graphene. After the invention of graphene to GQDs, researchers have discovered of functional conversion give effects on GQDs. For example, the hydrophilic character of carboxylic acid (COOH) functional groups have given positive impacts which is a stable and strong structure of graphene. Therefore, COOH functionalized is an excellent option for adjusting the performances of materials [7].

The potential of carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots (CGQDs) thin film will be greatly developed when the composite is in combination with polymeric materials [8]. An organic polymeric material with good optical and mechanical properties has attracted attention from the scientific community [9]. Chitosan is one of organic polymeric material that consists of the copolymer of glucosamine and N-acetyl glucosamine linked by a β-1,4 glycosidic bond which been derived by alkaline N-deacetylation from chitin [10]. It has been used in several different fields as promising optical materials. Other than that, the special characteristics of chitosan are biodegradability, biocompatibility, good film-forming, and good absorption properties, low cost and non-toxicity [2]. In order to increase the absorption performance, chitosan derivatives and composites have been extensively explored. Hence, chitosan has been integrated with CGQDs as a thin film to be used in an optical sensor to detect heavy metal ion.

Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) spectroscopy, one of the optical sensor has been developed in various configuration and formats for sensing a variety of target samples, including heavy metal ion [11–13]. This optical sensor is a surface-sensitive technique that been used to characterize the thickness and refractive index of the dielectric medium. This technique was widely used in biological and chemical sensor applications [14–18]. This optical process involved an interaction between a thin metal film and dielectric material that will propagate a polarized light. A sharp shadow, called surface plasmon resonance, is observed by the reduction of the intensity of the reflected light at a specific incident angle, which is due to the resonance energy between the incident beam and surface plasmon wave [19]. By developing or depositing a thin film of suitable material on the metal thin film, it will be resulting in a good specificity of the SPR optical sensor to heavy metal ion [20–22].

Although fruitful accomplishments have been achieved worldwide, the study of optical properties of chitosan/carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots (Cs/CGQDs) thin film and its surface plasmon resonance sensing properties is yet to be reported. Therefore in this study, the optical and potential sensing properties of Cs/CGQDs thin film are explored and reported.
The CGQDs solution was purchased from ASC Material, America. Chitosan and acetic acid were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA).

Firstly, 1 ml of stock acetic acid was diluted by adding deionized water in 100 ml volumetric flask to obtain 1% acetic acid. Next, 50 ml of 1% aqueous acetic acid was used to dissolve 400 mg medium molecular weight chitosan. Then, the solution was stirred to ensure powdered chitosan dissolved completely. Lastly, CGQDs solution was obtained by mixing 10 ml of diluted CGQDs into chitosan solution and stirred.

Firstly, the glass cover slip (
24 × 24 × 0.1
mm3) was cleaned by acetone to remove any dirt or fingerprint mark on the surface of the glass film. Next, the glass slip was coated with gold layer by using SC7640 Sputter Coater for 67 s.

Then, the glass slip underwent another coating process to add another layer. 1000 μl of diluted chitosan solution, diluted CGQDs solution and diluted Cs/CGQDs solution were added separately on gold-coated glass cover slip and spun under 3000 rev/min for 30 s. Spin coater was used to ensure the glass slip surface coated with chitosan, CGQDs, and Cs/CGQDs solution evenly.

FTIR test was conducted by using Perkin-Elmer spectrophotometer (CA, USA) in setting wavelength range of 400–4000 cm−1. UV–vis-NIR spectroscopy (UV-3600 Shimadzu) was used to determine the absorbance of the samples in the range of 200–500 nm in room temperature. Photoluminescence spectra was obtained using L5 55 Luminescence Spectrometer. For AFM measurement, Q-scope 250, Qesant Instrument Corporation was used in intermittent mode to obtain high resolution for a fragile sample such as thin film.

For sensing metal ion, SPR measurement has been carried out to determine the potential of Cs/CGQDs thin film for sensing Hg2+ solution. Fig. 1 shows the schematic diagram of the SPR instrument setup. The SPR measurement was carried out by measuring the reflected He-Ne laser beam (632.8 nm, 5 mW) as a function of incident angle. The SPR setup consists of a He-Ne laser, an optical stage driven by a stepper motor with a resolution of 0.001° (Newport MM 3000), a polarizer and an optical chopper (SR 540). The He-Ne laser beam was incident on the prism (refractive index of 1.77861), passed through the thin film and a photodiode. The reflected beam was detected and then processed by the lock-in amplifier (SR 530) [23].

The FTIR spectrum for chitosan, CGQDs, and Cs/CGQDs thin film are shown in Fig. 2 in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The spectrum of chitosan thin film possesses broad absorption band at 3456 cm−1 which can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH bond. In addition, a weak CH bond of chitosan also can be found at 2892 cm−1 and other absorption bands at 1644 cm−1, 1402 cm−1 are associated with the presence of the CO stretching bond. At 1074 cm−1, represents the absorption peak for CO group, that indicates the presence of the COOH group in chitosan thin film.

In the spectrum of CGQDs thin film, there is an absorption band at 3310 cm−1 owing to the OH bond of CGQDs. Then, the peak at 2951 cm−1 is due to a weaker CH bond and there are three characteristic absorbance bands centered at 1625 cm−1, 1417 cm−1 and 1107 cm−1, which correspond to the CO stretch of the carboxylic group.

The spectrum of Cs/CGQDs thin film exhibits a combination of characteristics similar to chitosan and CGQDs which is resulted in the absorption peak at 3308 cm−1 related to OH absorbance vibration. The peak at 2915 cm−1 is characteristics of the CH bond and CO stretching vibrations can be observed at the peak of 162 cm−1 and 1412 cm−1, followed by CO bond at 1084 cm−1.

The results of this investigation show that chitosan, CGQDs, and Cs/CGQDs thin film exhibit same infrared spectrum of the absorption peak of OH bond, CH bond, CO bond, and CO bond. Table 1 represents the characteristics frequencies of Cs/CGQDs thin film.

For the optical properties analysis, the absorbance spectrum of the thin film was measured by the wavelength range of 200–500 nm. Fig. 3 presents the ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of chitosan, CGQDs, and Cs/CGQDs thin film.

The absorbance spectrum has different absorbance value for each thin film. From the graph, the absorption spectrum of the Cs/CGQDs thin film is the highest, where at approximately 265 nm–275 nm, two absorbance peak with the value of 5.99 and 5.97 are observed. It is probably due to the presence of π→π* bond transitions of the carbonyl groups in composite thin film [24,25].

To determine the absorbance of UV–vis absorbance spectrum, the theory by Beer Lambert law is used. According to Beer Lambert law, the concentration of substances in solution is directly proportional to absorbance. It also describes the relationship between absorbance and transmittance. This law is only true for monochromatic light. When monochromatic radiation passes through a homogeneous solution, the intensity of the emitted radiation depends on thickness, t and concentration of the solution.

The absorbance, A of samples depends on the ratio of intensities of the illumination falling on the detector in the absence of Iₒ and the presence It of the sample.
(1)
A =
log 10
I 0
I t

The transmittance, T of a sample is given by:
(2)
T =
I t
I ∘

Absorbance and transmittance are related, thus:
(3)
A =
log 10
T

Another quantity that can be measured is the absorbance coefficient. It is a useful quantity when comparing samples of a varying thickness. The absorbance coefficient is given as
(4)
α = 2.303
A t

In this equation, t is the thickness of the sample in units of metre (m) and consequently, the absorbance coefficient α is to be reported in units of m−1. In spectroscopy, the absorbance coefficient is directly derived from the illumination of an intensity of light at a specified wavelength (λ) that passes through a sample (transmitted light intensity) and Iₒ is the intensity of the light before it enters the sample or incident light intensity.

In addition, by the help of absorption spectra, the energy band gap of these composites can be calculated. The Tauc relation is used in order to calculate the optical band gap energy from absorption spectra,
(5)
α =
k
( h v −
E g
)
1 2
h v
By rearrangement of the above equation,
(6)
( α h v )
2
= k ( h v −
E g
)
Where hv is the photon energy, h is Plank’s constant, α is the absorption coefficient, Eg is the optical energy gap, k is constant and for direct transitions n = 1/2. When a graph (αhv) 2 against hv is plotted, the intersection of this straight line on x-axis gives the value of optical band gap, Eg [26].

Figs. 4–6
show the variations of optical band gap energies of different thin film. The graph of (αhv) 2 as a function of hv of chitosan, CGQDs, and Cs/CGQDs, gives energy band gap, Eg value of 4.055 eV, 3.955 eV and 4.012 eV respectively. The variations of optical band gap energies due to chitosan solution as Cs/CGQDs thin film energy band gap is higher than CGQDs thin film. The zero band gap of graphene quantum dots can be modified by adjusting the surface chemistry and the size of the dots [27]. An experiment shows that the band gap of GQDs can increase to approximately 2 eV for aromatic rings and 7 eV for benzene [28]. Table 2 represents the energy band gap for three composite thin films.

Fig. 7 shows the photoluminescence intensity of chitosan, CGQDs, and Cs/CGQDs thin film emission at an excitation wavelength of 250 nm. Based on Fig. 7, chitosan thin film indicated an intense emission peak at wavelength 742 nm.

Next, the photoluminescence spectra CGQDs thin film showed an intense emission peak at 748 nm and Cs/CGQDs thin film was around 748 nm. In conclusion, red light emission spectra by three thin films are emitted due to the absorption peak about 300 nm corresponds to π→π* transitions of CO bond which enhanced to recombination of trapped excitons leads to the variation and red shift of emission [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: SPR signal for Hg2+ on gold single layer
ce:section.ce:para: Prior measurement, a preliminary SPR test was conducted for a gold thin film in contact with deionized water. The deionized water was injected into the cell in contact with the gold thin film. Fig. 8 shows that the SPR reflectivity curve for a gold thin film in contact with deionized water and the resonance angle obtained is 54.10°.

Then, the SPR experiment was conducted by using different concentration of Hg2+ solutions which the concentration of 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 ppm were injected one after another into the cell to contact with the gold thin film. The resonance angle for all concentration was monitored and recorded. The results match those observed in earlier studies that there were no changes in the resonance angle for any metal with different concentrations when in contact with the gold thin film [30]. A possible explanation for these results may due to the small amount of Hg2+ exists in the concentration solution that was absorbed to the gold surface. Therefore, it can be affected by the similarity of the refractive index for low concentration of heavy metal ion [19,31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: SPR signal for Hg2+ using Cs/CGQDs on gold surface
ce:section.ce:para: The SPR experiment for the detection of Hg2+ was continued by replacing gold thin film with gold/Cs/CGQDs thin film. The SPR experiment was first repeated with deionized water. The deposited Cs/CGQDs on gold thin film had changes on resonance angle, where the resonance angle is increased and was shifted to the right. The resonance angle for gold/Cs/CGQDs in contact with deionized water is 55.51° compared to 54.10° when in contact with the gold thin film. It can therefore be assumed that the changes of resonance angle due to the change refractive index of the thin film when Cs/CGQDs thin film was coated on gold thin film. Fig. 9 shows the SPR curve for gold/Cs/CGQDs in contact with deionized water to compare the results on the different concentration of Hg2+ ion.

Then, the SPR experiment was carried out by using the different concentration of Hg2+ solutions which the concentration of 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 ppm was injected one after another into the cell. The resonance angle for all concentration was monitored and recorded. From the SPR curve in Fig. 10 (a) and (b), it is apparent that there is a slight shift of the resonance angle with the increasing concentration of the Hg2+ solution.

The lower concentration of Hg2+ ion solution was used to compare and find the detection limit of the Cs/CGQDs thin film by comparing the SPR reflectivity curve of the Cs/CGQDs thin film with deionized water. Data from Fig. 10(a) compared different concentration which shows the resonance angle of the 0.5 ppm Hg2+ has a slight shift from the resonance angle of gold/Cs/CGQDs in contact with deionized water. Taken together, these results suggest that the detection limit of Cs/CGQDs thin film is at 0.5 ppm of Hg2+ ion. There is a significant positive correlation between the concentrations of Hg2+ ion as the angle shift can be observed.

The observation could be attributed by an interaction of Hg2+and Cs/CGQDs thin film leading to the formation of a pair of shared electrons between the positive charge from the Hg2+ ion and negative charge from the amine functional group in chitosan. GQDs enhance important roles in increasing the optical absorption in both the visible and infrared ranges. In summary, these results show that the presence of Cs/CGQDs thin film improves the sensitivity as compared to the gold thin film.

When Hg2+ concentrations in contact with the gold thin film there are no changes in resonance angle (Δ θ = 0) for as shown in Fig. 11 . As the Cs/CGQDs coated on gold thin film, the resonance angle charges. The values of shift of resonance angle have calculated and recorded. Figs. 11 and 12 show the comparison between the shift of resonance angle for gold thin film and gold/Cs/CGQDs thin film in contact with different concentration of Hg2+ in the range 10 ppm–100 ppm. Linear progression analysis produces the slope of 0.00062° ppm−1. Overall, this result indicates that optical sensor based on Cs/CGQDs thin film to detect Hg2+ ion have a sensitivity of 0.00062° ppm−1.
The microscopic characteristic of CGQD was identified by atomic force microscopy (AFM) working in tapping mode.

Fig. 13 (a) and (b) show AFM images of Cs/CGQDs thin film before and after in contact with mercury solution. Fig. 13(a) shows the granular structure with an average height of 18.4 nm. Fig. 13(b) shows nanoneedles have perfect sharp with an average height 10.1 nm. After treated with mercury solution, the numbers of nanoneedles and the granular structure increase with small roughness. There is an increment in thickness of the thin film after in contact with Hg2+ solution due to Hg2+ solution has covered thin film evenly. However, there is no clear distinction of a surface thin film for all composites.

In this study, Cs/CGQDs thin film has been prepared using spin coating technique. The results from FTIR indicated the interaction between chitosan and CGQDs. The UV–vis spectroscopy results proved that absorption of the Cs/CGQD quantum dots thin film has the highest absorbance value with an optical band gap of 4.012 eV. From photoluminescence spectroscopy, red light emission spectra indicated π→π* transitions of CO bond where absorption peak found at around 725 nm to 775 nm. The microscopic characteristic of the Cs/CGQDs thin film before and after in contact Hg2+ solution was identified. AFM confirmed that Cs/CGQDs thin film has a granular structure with an average height of 18.4 nm. The experimental SPR curves were obtained for different concentration (0 to 100 ppm) Hg2+ solution. The shifts of resonance angle shows a linear relationship for Cs/CGQDs thin film in contact with Hg2+ solution with a sensitivity of 0.00062° ppm−1. These results confirmed that the Cs/CGQDs thin film has the potential for the detection of Hg2+ using surface plasmon resonance technique.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledged the financial support for this study from the Universiti Putra Malaysia (UPM) through Putra Grant (9531500). The laboratory facilities provided by the Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia are also acknowledged.
